text: In the laparoscopic splenectomy, the spleen is maneuvered forward to expose the hilum, allowing for the safe division and ligation of the vessels.
The surgeon divides the splenic artery before approaching the spleen. The lesser sac is opened by incising the gastro splenic ligament, and the short gastric vessels are clipped and divided.
The surgeon isolates the splenic pedicle and divides the splenic artery using absolute locking PDS clips, ensuring secure closure of the vessel.

The surgeon uses a flexible curved retractor to lift the lower edge of the spleen, facilitating dissection along the lower pole. Individual vessels are clipped and divided as the dissection progresses towards the hilum, with the splenic artery remaining intact.
The surgeon repeats the clipping process at the splenic end of the vessel before dividing it, ensuring secure vessel management prior to further dissection.

The surgeon uses curved forceps with a flexible shaft to dissect a larger vessel with diathermy. Petal and forceps are maneuvered around the back of the vessel to ensure it is clear, and a catgut ligature is tied around one end of the vessel externally.
The surgeon narrows the diameter of the vessel, allowing it to be securely clipped with large metal clips before being divided.

The surgeon continues dissection towards the hilum of the spleen, elevating the lower pole with a flexible curved retractor. Vessels and peritoneal attachments are stretched and either dissected or divided, allowing adjacent structures to visibly fall away from the spleen.

The surgeon opens the gastrosplenic ligament to gain control of the hilum, passing curved forceps under the remaining pedicle. A linear thread lifts the pedicle, while a curved retractor draws the tail of the pancreas medially. The pedicle is assessed with a sizer for thickness before being divided with an endoscopic GIA stapler.
The surgeon continues dissection close to the spleen, clipping and dividing individual branches of the splenic vessels after the early division of the splenic artery.

The surgeon uses a retractor to lift the spleen away from its attachments, revealing the lino-renal ligament. A second retractor applies counter traction, sweeping intraperitoneal structures medially. Diathermy dissection is performed with tissues held under tension.

Under magnification, the surgeon clips small branches of the splenic artery individually while dissecting around the hilum. The tail of the pancreas is visible near the spleen, with care taken to avoid damage during the procedure.

The surgeon lifts the spleen to divide the posterior leaf of the linoleum ligament, the last remaining attachment. The spleen is then placed into a tough plastic intravenous infusion bag, which is inserted into the abdomen through a 25mm cannula. The edges of the bag are brought up to the surface through the cannula.

The surgeon breaks up the spleen digitally within the intravenous infusion bag and removes it in several pieces using sponge holding forceps. The suspended pedicle technique is utilized during the laparoscopic splenectomy.
